observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE instruct the:
a. MA to (i) adjust stale checks amounting ₱195,845.55 to correct balances of the
CIB-LCCA and the corresponding payable account; (ii) conduct regular
reconciliation of accounting ledgers and cashbooks, at least on a quarterly
basis, to promptly identify and resolve discrepancies, if any; and (iii) record
the balance of unreported bank account of ₱22,005.00 and ensure that all local
currency accounts of the LGU are duly recognized in the books; and
b. Municipal Treasurer (MT) to (i) locate the payees of the staled checks and
inform them of the check status to facilitate the necessary actions for accounts
adjustments; (ii) send written notice to the concerned payees at least one
month before a check becomes stale and ensure proper cancellation and
reporting of stale checks; (iii) record the identified reconciling items in the
cashbook; (iv) investigate the sources of the deposits and transaction history
of the newly opened bank account; and (v) regularly coordinate with the MAO
to ensure all accounts are accurately reported.

We recommended that the LCE:
a. ensure that the approved bond amount aligns with the CAs granted to SDO
and refrain from granting additional CAs if the previous ones were not fully
settled or accounted for. Likewise, consider increasing the amount of the bond
of the MT or designating an additional SDO; and
b. instruct the MA to (i) strictly monitor the CAs granted by requiring the SDOs
to liquidate their CAs including the unspent balances in their possession within
the reglementary period; (ii) ensure that the disbursements are duly supported
with complete documentation; and (iii) reconcile the balances of the
outstanding CAs per GL, Progress Report on the Settlement of Cash
Advances, and related journals.

We recommended that Management undertake the following courses of
action:
a. instruct the MA to (i) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to
correct the recording of advance payments of RPT and SET:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Other Deferred Credits ₱4,051,112.36
Discount on Advance Payments 894,789.76
Deferred RPT ₱2,472,951.06
Deferred SET 2,472,951.06
(ii) regularly set up the RPT and SET Receivables and corresponding Deferred
RPT and SET at the beginning of the year; (iii) make use of the Certified List
of Taxpayers and Taxes Due to be submitted by the MT as the basis for setting
up the RPT and SET Receivables and Deferred Credits at the beginning of the
year; and (iv) trace and record the necessary adjustments to reconcile the
discrepancies between the amount of the recorded RPT and SET Receivables
and the related Deferred Credits accounts; and
b. instruct the MT to (i) expedite the updating of List of Delinquent Real
Property Taxpayers; (ii) continuously monitor receivables by sending demand
letters on the delinquent accounts and intensify efforts on collection to increase
RPT and SEF collection efficiency; (iii) consider the other tax remedies
provided in R.A. No. 7160 such as posting the notice of the delinquency at the
main entrance of the Municipal Hall and in publicly accessible and
conspicuous places in each barangay of the LGU concerned; and (iv) furnish
the MA of a duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amounts
due and collectible for the year to serve as the basis of recording.

We recommended that the LCE:
a. instruct the MA to (i) conduct a thorough review of the Inventory accounts
and reconcile them with the records of the concerned Offices; (ii) derecognize
items that have already been consumed or distributed to end-users in a timely
manner; and (iii) maintain SLC for each type of inventory items to record the
acquisition, issuance and disposal.
b. instruct the MT and other concerned offices to (a) maintain SC for each item
in stock; and (b) prepare SSMI to be submitted every week or at least once a
month to the MA together with RIS or equivalent document so that issuances
will be computed and recorded by the latter; and
c. create an Inventory Committee that would facilitate the conduct of physical
count of inventory items by type semestrally and will prepare the
corresponding RPCI.

We recommended that the LCE:
a. advise the MA to draw the necessary journal entries to correct the improper
recording of newly purchased PPEs and adjust the improperly recorded
depreciation of various PPEs;
b. create an Inventory Committee who will spearhead the conduct of the annual
physical count of PPE and require the same to prepare and submit the
RPCPPE to the AT not later than January 31 of each year. Likewise, strategize
the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE account balances pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2020-006; and
c. direct the Property Officer to (i) prioritize the registration/renewal of all
motor vehicles owned by the Municipality with the LTO to prevent possible
loss and misuse of the assets and to avoid incurring penalties, fines or sanctions
from the LTO; and (ii) facilitate the insurance of all motor vehicles owned by
the Municipality to ensure the LGU is indemnified or compensated for any
damage or loss due to possible casualties that may happen on these insurable
assets.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the MAO to:
a. draw the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the recording of two
infrastructure projects:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
CIP – Infrastructure Assets ₱996,808.37
Other Infrastructure Assets ₱996,808.37
Particulars Dr. Cr.
To correct the double recording of the transfer of the project Construction of
Multi-Purpose Building - Stage at Barangay Balincaguing upon completion.
Particulars Dr. Cr.
CIP – Infrastructure Assets ₱596,924.84
CIP - Buildings and Other Structures ₱596,924.84
To correct the improper recording of the payment/transfer of the project
Concreting of Sagpat - Maloma Road at Barangay Maloma.
b. locate pertinent records to trace the details of the CIP accounts, reclassify the
completed projects to appropriate asset accounts and provide the
corresponding depreciation; and
c. maintain the appropriate CIPLC for each specific type of asset constructed,
whether through a straight contract or by administration.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
a. MA to (i) coordinate with the HRMA relative to the earned leave credits of
Municipal employees; (ii) draw a JEV to record the initial set-up of provision
for earned leave credits in the books of accounts of the ensuing year; and (iii)
ensure that all claims for TLB are duly supported with the necessary
documentary requirements provided under COA Circular No. 2012-001; and
b. HRMA to (i) revisit its process of maintaining and reporting employee leave
records, which should include updating and encoding of employee leave cards
regularly, ensuring the retrieval and attachment of complete employee leave
records when processing claims, and other internal control measures to
provide reliable information; and (ii) provide Schedules of Earned Leave
Credits to MA to serve as basis for recognition of LBP in the books.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the MA to:
a. undertake a one-time cleansing of the Due to NGAs account by following the
guidelines and procedures for the write-off of dormant accounts, specifically
those from unknown SA;
b. regularly update the SL to ensure accurate tracking of transactions;
c. make representations with the identified SA to facilitate the proper liquidation
and/or return of unutilized fund transfers;
d. prepare the necessary Journal Entry Vouchers to adjust the erroneous entries
and correct the improperly recorded transactions; and
e. cause the execution/submit the MOA for all fund transfers which shall contain
the guidelines and procedures in the implementation of projects by the
implementing/recipient LGUs, including their responsibilities.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator (MPDC) and the Municipal Engineer properly plan and closely monitor
the implementation of the development projects to prevent the incurrence of delay;
(b) Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the contractors to ensure regular updating
of the project status to comply with the guidelines on information and publicity on
PPAs; (c) Bids and Awards Committee require the winning contractor to submit their
DOLE-approved CSHP within the prescribed period; and (d) MA to submit the
unsubmitted DVs and supporting documents immediately to facilitate the audit of the
transactions.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
a. LDRRM Officer to (i) closely monitor and ensure that the LDRRM planned
PPAs are implemented and give due regard on the maximum utilization of the
70% MF for pre-disaster preparedness programs so that the intended purpose
is attained/achieved; and (ii) include in the MDRRMFIP the list of programs,
projects and activities to be undertaken out of the unexpended LDRRMF of
previous years under Special Trust Fund; and
b. MA to perform a thorough review of all disbursement claims and ensure only
those with complete documentation are processed and presented for payment.

We recommended that Management:
a. instruct the HRMA to (i) execute JO contracts with the JO personnel to clearly
define their duties and responsibilities and terms of employment; (ii) provide
the AT with copies of the Appointment of Casual personnel;
b. advise the MA and MT to ensure that the claims for services of Casual and JO
personnel are supported with complete and appropriate documentation; and
c. refrain from paying traveling expenses or approving training participation for
JO personnel unless all conditions specified under Section 3(a) of EO No. 77 s.
of 2019 and COA Resolution No. 2021-044 dated December 28, 2021 are fully
satisfied.

We recommended that the LCE to:
a. instruct the LGU GFPS to monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs to
ensure that the same are implemented as planned to attain GAD’s goals and
objectives;
b. issue an appropriate policy directive, copy furnished to the DILG Office that
conducted the review of the LGU GPB, to ensure the dissemination and
implementation of the GPB; and shall mandate the LGU GFPS to: a) provide
technical assistance in the implementation of the GPB; b) monitor its
implementation; c) ensure the preparation and submission of GAD AR; and
d) consolidate reports on the implementation of the GPB; and
c. instruct the GFP to submit the documentary requirements supporting the
GAD Accomplishment Report.

We recommended that the LCE direct the MA and the MT including all
Accountable Officers to (a) strictly adhere to the timeframes on the submission of
accounts and financial reports; (b) immediately transmit the unsubmitted DVs and
supporting documents; and (c) impose appropriate sanctions against those
officials/employees concerned who have continuously disregarded the set deadline for
the submission of the accounts/reports.

We commended Management for complying with its duties as withholding and
remitting agent of taxes in accordance with RMC No. 23-2012 dated February 14,
2012. Nevertheless, we recommended that the MAO (a) prepare the necessary JEV to
correct the remaining misposted transactions and be more careful in the recording of
withholding taxes to avoid erroneous posting thereof; and (b) strictly monitor and
determine the exact amount to be remitted each month to avoid under/over over-
remittances.

We commended the Management for its substantial compliance with the
requirements of R.A. No. 8291. However, we recommended that the MAO be more
careful in the recording of premium contributions and loan amortizations to avoid
under recording/misposting thereof and trace/reconcile the details of the remaining
balance of ₱294,833.22. Moreover, strictly monitor and determine the exact amount
to be remitted each month to avoid under/over remittances.

We commended the Management for substantially complying with the
withholding and remittances of withheld premiums and loan amortizations within the
prescribed period.

Nevertheless, we recommended that the MAO:
(i) prepare the necessary JEV to correct the erroneous recording as follows:
Account Title Dr. Cr.
Due to Pag-IBIG ₱613,072.88
Due to GSIS 118,802.80
Due to PhilHealth ₱142,970.18
Due to GOCCs 588,905.50
(ii) trace and reconcile the details of unremitted balances from prior years and
immediately remit the same to avoid the imposition of penalties.

We commended the Management for its continuous compliance with the
provisions of R.A. No. 10606 on proper deduction and remittance of PhilHealth
contributions.

Nevertheless, we recommended that the MAO (i) facilitate the reconciliation
of the over remittance to ensure an accurate balance of the Due to PhilHealth account
at year-end; and (ii) prepare the necessary JEV to correct the erroneous recording as
follows:
Account Title Dr. Cr.
Due to PhilHealth ₱ 21,475.10
Due to Pag-IBIG 142,970.18
Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable ₱164,445.28
We recommended that both offices continue the reconcilation of CLT and CIB accounts.
observations.managementAction: Not The presence of various reconciling items can be attributed to the lack of periodic reconciliation between the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) and the Municipal Treasurer’s Office (MTO).

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):
a. refrain from granting additional CAs if the previous ones were not fully settled or accounted for. Consider possible withholding of salaries of those officials and employees who persistently refuse to liquidate their CAs within the timeframe as required by the present regulation; b. instruct the MA to (i) strictly monitor the CAs granted by requiring the SDOs to liquidate their CAs including the unspent balances in their possession within the reglementary period; (ii) send Demand Letter to the concerned officials and employees with unliquidated CAs; (iii) reconcile the balances of the outstanding CAs per GL, Progress Report on the Settlement of CAs, and related journals; (iv) ensure that the liquidation of CAs were duly recorded in the book; and c. instruct the concerned officials and employees to liquidate their CAs within the reglementary period.
observations.managementAction: The MAO continuously reminded those personnel with unliquidated CAs verbally.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MA ensure full compliance with the deadline of submission of the CA and after the release of supporting documents for audit.
observations.managementAction: The CA DVs were already submitted to the COA Auditor’s Office within seven days after the release of the check.

observations.recommendation:

The committee report shall be the basis of their recommendation to the LCE, who in turn shall request for the disposal of unserviceable properties to COA in accordance with Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 125 of the MNGAS, Volume I; and d. regularly renew the registration of all serviceable motor vehicles of the Municipality with LTO and contracts of insurance with GSIS to avoid additional expenses in terms of penalties and payment of damages to third parties in case of accidents.

observations.managementAction: Not Implemented. There were still delays observed in the submission of accounts and financial reports of the Municipality. A total of 271 DVs with an aggregate amount of ₱41,896,996.27 remained unsubmitted as of February 28, 2025. As of May 31, 2025, out of 271 unsubmitted accounts, a total of 60 DVs with aggregate amount of ₱25,562,024.21 have been submitted for audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the LCE to: a. advise the LO to (i) submit a representation to the SB to set up or institutionalize the use of PILAA to arrive at a more realistic basis of business taxes in the absence of the required audited financial statements, ITRs or Sworn Statement of Gross Sales or Receipts; and documentary evidence supporting the gross sales or receipts. (ii) require the submission of complete documentation of the financial records from taxpayers for the correct assessment of business taxes; and b. instruct the MT to (i) regularly examine the books of accounts of business establishments to determine the truthfulness of the declared gross sales or receipts used in the computation of business taxes paid pursuant to Section 171 of R.A. No. 7160; (ii) assign personnel at the MTO who are well-equipped with the necessary knowledge and training in the examination of books of accounts to enable the said Office to carry on with their mandate efficiently; and (iii) tag all the afore-cited suppliers and contractors with lower sales or receipts declared and be subject to examination of books of accounts to ensure the correctness of taxes, fees, and charges to be collected.
observations.managementAction: Taxpayers are required to submit sworn statements of their gross sales or receipts for the quarter to verify the actual sales. Payments were already made by taxpayers under OR Nos. 12723066 and 12722870 both dated May 02, 2024 amounting to ₱5,747.00 and ₱22,384.00, respectively. Personnel are being considered to be assigned/hired exclusively for the purpose of conducting examinations but no personnel have been assigned/hired exclusively for the purpose as of CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: a. strictly implement the mandatory segregation of wastes at source through (i) enacting an ordinance that would impose fines and penalties for violators; and (ii) conducting an extensive information education campaign that would educate and encourage the active participation of the constituents particularly the market vendors on proper waste segregation; b. ensure that waste collectors are equipped with personal protective equipment to free them from hazards of handling solid wastes; and c. expedite the purchase of vehicles specifically designed for the collection, transport, and disposal of solid wastes.
observations.managementAction: The MSWMO submitted the final draft of Comprehensive Municipal Ecological Solid Waste Management Ordinance to the Sangguniang Bayan. The Municipality purchased one unit garbage compactor truck for garbage/waste collection per DV No. 2024-06-1546 dated June 20, 2024. Protective gears were purchased and the LCE instructed the wearing thereof. No documents were submitted to support the conduct of extensive information education campaign; the Municipality is doing its best to educate market vendors.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the GFPS: a. strive for the full implementation of GAD-related PPAs by utilizing judiciously the budget set aside for said purposes; and b. ensure that GAD AR is supported with the requirements mentioned under Section 4.C.8.5 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person and GAD TWG Chairperson committed to comply with the audit recommendations. Some GAD activities totaling ₱198,085.00 were not implemented due to reasons such as lack of staff, focus on other projects, non-referral of clients, and barangays not submitting lists of identified persons who use drugs. The GAD AR was uploaded to GAD-PBMS and returned by DILG for revisions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):
a. refrain from granting additional CAs if the previous ones were not fully
settled or accounted for. Consider possible withholding of salaries of those
officials and employees who persistently refuse to cooperate and did not
liquidate their CAs within the timeframe as required by the present
regulation;
b. instruct the MA to (i) strictly monitor the CAs granted by requiring the
personnel concerned to liquidate their CAs including the unspent balances
in their possession within the reglementary period; (ii) send Demand Letter
to officials and employees with unliquidated CAs; (iii) reconcile the
balances of the outstanding CAs per GL, Progress Report on the
Settlement of CAs, and related journals; and (iv) ensure that the
liquidation of CAs were duly recorded in the book; and
c. instruct the concerned officials and employees to liquidate their CAs within
the reglementary period.

However, we recommended that the MA ensure full
compliance with the deadline of submission of the CA and supporting documents for
audit.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the MA to:
a. regularly update the SL to ensure accurate tracking of transactions;
b. make representations with the identified SA to facilitate the proper
liquidation and/or return of unutilized fund transfers;
c. undertake a one-time cleansing of the Due to NGAs account by following
the guidelines and procedures for the write-off of dormant accounts,
specifically those from unknown SA; and
d. prepare the necessary JEVs to adjust the erroneous entries and correct the
improperly recorded transactions.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the MA to:
a. assign additional staff to assist the personnel in charge in regularly
updating the SL to ensure accurate tracking of transactions;
b. undertake a one-time cleansing of the Due to LGUs account by adhering
to the guidelines and procedures prescribed under COA Circular No.
2023-008; and
c. ensure the timely remittance of respective shares of the Provincial
Government and component barangays in RPT collections.

Nonetheless, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer and
the MPDC closely monitor the ongoing projects to prevent the incurrence of delay in
the completion of projects.

We recommended that the LCE:
a. direct the MDRRM Officer to (i) closely monitor and ensure that the
LDRRM planned PPAs are implemented and give due regard on the
maximum utilization of the 70% MF for pre-disaster preparedness
programs so that the intended purpose is attained/achieved; (ii) include in
the MDRRMFIP the list of programs, projects and activities to be
undertaken out of the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years under STF;
and (iii) furnish the Office of the Auditor with Monthly Report on Sources
and Utilization of DRRMF within the reglementary period; and
b. MA to (i) transfer the unexpended balance of the DRRMF-MOOE and
QRF in the STF at year-end; (ii) revert the unutilized balance aged more
than five years of ₱531,557.45 to the unappropriated surplus of the GF;
and (iii) perform a thorough review of all disbursement claims and ensure
only those with complete documentation are processed and presented for
payment.

We recommended that Management direct the MA and the MT including all
AOs to (a) strictly adhere to the timeframes on the submission of accounts and
financial reports; and (b) immediately submit the aforementioned unsubmitted DVs
and supporting documents.

We recommended the LCE to:
a. advise the LO to (i) submit a representation to the SB to set up or
institutionalize the use of PILAA to arrive at a more realistic basis of
business taxes in the absence of the required audited financial statements,
ITRs or Sworn Statement of Gross Sales or Receipts; and (ii) require the
submission of complete documentation of the financial records from
taxpayers for the correct assessment of business taxes; and
b. instruct the MT to (i) regularly examine the books of accounts of business
establishments to determine the truthfulness of the declared gross sales or
receipts used in the computation of business taxes paid pursuant to Section
171 of R.A. No. 7160; (ii) assign personnel at the MTO who are well-
equipped with the necessary knowledge and training in the examination of
books of accounts to enable the said Office to carry on with their mandate
efficiently; and (iii) tag all the afore-cited suppliers and contractors with
lower sales or receipts declared and be subject to examination of books of
accounts to ensure the correctness of taxes, fees, and charges to be
collected.

We recommended that management:
a. strictly implement the mandatory segregation of wastes at source through
(i) enacting an ordinance that would impose fines and penalties for
violators; and (ii) conducting an extensive information education
campaign that would educate and encourage the active participation of
the constituents particularly the market vendors on proper waste
segregation;
b. ensure that waste collectors are equipped with personal protective
equipment to free them from hazards of handling solid wastes; and
c. expedite the purchase of vehicles specifically designed for the collection,
transport, and disposal of solid wastes.

We recommended that the GFPS:
a. strive for the full implementation of GAD-related PPAs by utilizing
judiciously the budget set aside for said purposes; and
b. ensure that GAD AR is supported with the requirements mentioned under
Section 4.C.8.5 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

Nevertheless, we recommended that the Accounting personnel in charge strictly
monitor and determine the exact amount to be remitted each month to avoid
under/over remittances. Likewise, prepare the necessary JEV to correct the under
recording of remittance on November 2023 amounting to ₱270.00.

However, we recommended that the MAO exert extra
effort to remit the unremitted premium contributions for CYs 2023 and prior years
of ₱303,790.28, prepare necessary JEV to correct the misposted transactions with the
net amount of ₱1,063.70, and trace the details of the remaining balance of
₱248,572.41. Moreover, strictly monitor and determine the exact amount to be
remitted each month to avoid under/over remittances.

We appreciated the Municipality’s substantial adherence with HDMF
Circular on prompt remittance of contributions and other amounts due to Pag-IBIG
which could be attributed to the positive effort of both the Accounting and Treasury
Office. Nevertheless, we recommended that the (a) MTO be extra careful in doing
online payment and ensure that the payment was completely process; and (b) MAO
to trace and reconcile the details of unremitted balances from prior years and
immediately remit the same to avoid imposition of penalty.

Nonetheless, we recommended that the (a) MA and the MT (i) coordinate with
each other to avoid late remittance of premium contributions, (ii) refrain from paying
interest on late remittance out of Municipal funds, and (b) Accounting Office facilitate
the reconciliation of the over remittance to ensure an accurate balance of the Due to
PhilHealth account at year-end.

only claims with complete documentation are processed and presented
for payment;

b. create an Inventory Committee who will spearhead the conduct of annual
physical count of PPE and require the same to prepare and submit the
RPCPPE to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year; and

c. require the Municipal Assessor or the designated LGU personnel to
expedite the titling of recorded parcel of land in compliance with Section
148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 and Section 39 of P.D. No. 1445 to
establish ownership thereof and to fully protect the interest of the
Municipality.

2.17 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to (i) exert effort in retrieving the documents of
unidentified payables or those without details in the SL, verify the
validity thereof thru sending confirmation letters to identified claimants
or creditors, pay the valid claims and revert to the General Fund all
dormant/long outstanding AP which have been outstanding for two
years or more and against which no actual claims have been filed or
which are not covered by perfected contracts on record; (ii) effect the
necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the transactions erroneously
booked as AP to ensure that accounts are properly presented in the
financial statements; and (iii) conduct periodic review to ensure that the
AP consists only of valid and legitimate claims against the Municipality;
and

b. MBO to discontinue the practice of obligating for
programs/projects/activities which are not covered by perfected
contracts, not yet implemented/delivered and/or unbilled.

3.10 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. AOs to prepare the monthly RAAF and the Municipal Treasurer
prepare the monthly CRAAF and submit the said monthly reports not
later than the 5th day of the ensuing month to the Office of the Auditor;
b. Revenue collectors particularly the RPT collectors to issue the pre-
numbered official receipts in strict numerical sequence and refrain from
using a new booklet until the previous one was fully issued and
accounted;
c. Municipal Treasurer to strictly monitor the receipts and issuances of AF,
and ensure that all issued AF were duly accounted and corresponding
collections were fully remitted.

timely accomplishment thereof for the benefit of the intended
beneficiaries;

required DTRs and Accomplishment Reports to facilitate the liquidation
of cash advance for OCA; and

c. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Bids and
Awards Committee (BAC) for the completion and submission of the
documentary deficiencies noted and ensure that disbursement vouchers
are supported with complete documentation before payments are made.

6.28 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the (a) Municipal Treasurer to
(i) continuously monitor receivables by sending demand letters on the delinquent
accounts and intensify efforts on collection to increase RPT and SEF collection
efficiency; (ii) consider the other tax remedies provided in R.A. No. 7160 such as
posting the notice of the delinquency at the main entrance of the Municipal Hall and
in publicly accessible and conspicuous places in each barangay of the LGU concerned;
publishing the notice of delinquency once a week or for two (2) consecutive weeks in
a newspaper of general circulation of the Municipality; and availing of all legal and
administrative remedies either by administrative action through a levy on real property
or judicial action as a last resort; and (iii) furnish the Municipal Accountant of a duly
certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible for
the year to serve as the basis of recording; and (b) direct the Municipal Accountant to
(i) make use of the Certified List of Taxpayers and Taxes Due to be submitted by the
Municipal Treasurer as the basis for setting up the RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred
Credits at the beginning of the year, and (ii) trace and record the necessary adjustments
to reconcile the discrepancies between the amount of the recorded RPT and SET
Receivables and the related Deferred Credits accounts of ₱6,118,925.97 as at year-end.

7.17 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to (i) ensure the timely remittance of barangay
and provincial shares in RPT in the succeeding quarters pursuant to
Sections 271 and 272 of R.A. No. 7160, and (ii) exert extra effort to
expedite the establishment of the total unremitted shares for prior years
and immediately remit the same to the 11 barangays and PGZ; and

b. Municipal Treasurer to submit the Schedule of Barangay and Provincial
Shares in RPT collections to the MAO immediately after the end of every
quarter.

8.36 We recommended that LCE instruct the:

a. HRMA to (i) execute a Job Order Contract with the JO personnel to
clearly define their duties and responsibilities and terms of employment;
(ii) implement stricter control mechanisms in the monitoring of the
attendance of casuals and JO personnel to ensure that they remain
productive in their respective area of assignments within the designated
time schedule of duty; (iii) revisit and evaluate the responsibilities and job
descriptions of employees to avoid possible duplication of work and
ensure that they are assigned to offices that are in dire need of their
services; (iv) assess the current Organizational Structure and Staffing
Pattern of the LGU so that the necessity and propriety of hiring the JO
personnel are justified; and (v) stop the practice of payment of overtime
to JO personnel in order to prevent the issuance of Notice of Disallowance
(ND); and

b. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer ensure that the claims
for services of casuals and JO personnel are supported with complete and
appropriate documentation.

payment.

continue its efforts in promoting and ensuring gender mainstreaming in local

evaluation as required by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended

15.9 We recognized the effort of Management for substantial remittance of

We recommended and the management agreed to (a) require every League of Local Government Units, and League and Federation of Local Elective Officials to submit complete supporting documents prior to any release of financial aid; (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the documents are complete before processing any claim thereof; and (c) stop any payment to private organizations where municipal officials/employees are members or affiliates thereto using the Municipal funds; otherwise, the Audit Team shall be constrained to disallow the same.

We recommended and the management agreed to (a) institute stricter control over the processing and releasing of payments of claims to ensure completeness of supporting documents and prompt and immediate rendition of monthly accounts; (b) submit the accounts and reports for various months and adhere strictly to the timeframes on the submission of accounts and financial reports to the Audit Team pursuant to Section 100 of P.D. No. 1445, and COA Circular No. 2009-006; and (c) see to it that all unsubmitted accounts (vouchers and official receipts) for CY 2016 are transmitted to the Office of the Auditor; otherwise, we shall be constrained to recommend the withholding of salaries of concerned Municipal officials/personnel.
We recommended that the management continually institute effective internal control to avoid audit disallowances/suspensions/charges in all its financial transactions and activities.

observations.managementAction:
The LCE required the OIC – Municipal Accountant to submit final report for the details of the unutilized balances.




During the CY 2016, the PDAF balances amounting to ₱11,800.67 were reverted. 




The Accounting Office prepared Liquidation Report for the a) implemented livelihood program funded by DOLE amounting to ₱200,000.00; and b) implemented projects funded in current years.




Management maintained copy of MOAs and TAs for the current fund transfers.
observations.reason:
There was no final report prepared for the unutilized PDAF and Other Trust Fund balances. But the Municipal Accountant was able to determine the details of the balance of five projects amounting to ₱11,800.67.




Details of other balances of PDAF were not yet determined by the OIC - Municipal Accountant.     




No reconciliation was conducted with the source agencies of funds.




As of December 31, 2016, there were still balances of fund transfers from the year 2015 and prior years of implemented projects yet to be liquidated.




Management failed to request or retrieve copy of MOA and TAs for the fund transfers in CY 2015 and prior years.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the management instruct the Accountant to obtain a copy of credit notices from Landbank of the Philippines for funds credited to the General Fund of the municipality received from National Government Agencies for proper recording in the books. 


Fund transfers from NGAs should be booked up in the Trust Fund.  Further, the account Due from NGAs should not be automatically debited for disbursements made, rather, only upon liquidation. 

Likewise, refrain from disbursing funds through cash advance and strictly observe the provisions of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction:
The OIC – Municipal Accountant was able to obtain a copy of credit notices from Landbank of the Philippines for funds credited to the General Fund.

Fund transfers for BUB projects were receipted and properly recorded in the Trust Fund.

Management avoided the drawing of cash advance in the disbursement of funds for BUB projects.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Accountant to ensure that the required documents for each type of transaction are submitted/attached to the DVs before their processing and payment. 

Further, submit all copies of contracts and purchase orders to the Audit Team within the prescribed period in order that timely evaluation be made thereof.

Likewise, (a) the Municipal Engineering Office prepare and submit PERT/CPM analysis and other required technical documents for every projects implemented by the LGU; 

(b) the BAC require the winning contractor to post performance security which is valid until issuance of the LGU of the final Certificate of Acceptance for the project; and


(c) ensure strict compliance with DOLE DO No. 13, Series of 1998 on the submission of construction safety and health program approved by DOLE for the five projects.
observations.managementAction:
Post audit of disburse-ments for concreting of roads disclosed that the management was already compliant with the audit requirements.




The PERT/CPM analysis and other required technical documents were prepared and submitted.

The BAC ensured that winning contractor either posted performance security or warranty security which is valid until the issuance of the LGU of the Final Certificate of Acceptance for the project.
observations.reason:
Copies of contracts and purchase orders were not submitted within five days after execution.




Management failed to submit the construction safety and health program approved by DOLE for the five projects.

observations.managementAction: The unexpended balance of 20% DF was considered continuing appropriation.
observations.reason:
Based on the plan and its utilization report on 20% DF, management still appropriated disbursed for 

PPAs not intended for development and those which are not capital in nature such as purchase of medical supplies, electrical supplies, meals, snacks, and office equipment, prizes for winners, and rental of tents and tables.

The proposal included non-capital investment projects such as of purchase of medical supplies, repair/ maintenance of street lights/power, livelihood program/ activities and vaccination/ deworming of animals.

Management was lax in the compliance with the guidelines for the planning and utilization of 20% Development Fund.

Reiterated under Observation No. 7 of Part II of the CY 2016 AAR.

observations.managementAction:
Management had already created the GFPS, Local GAD Code and Executive Committee.




The VAW Desk was established in every barangay.




The Focal Person submitted the annual GPB to the PPDO of the Provincial Government and DILG Regional Office.




Management aligned the PPAs charged to GAD as per COA Circular No. 2014-001.  There were no special projects or new PPAs that required the submission of report to the PCW.
observations.reason:
As per audit, management aligned the PPAs but actual utilization disclosed some expenses not related to GAD.








As per audit, management spent meals and snacks for various municipal activities, traveling expenses, cell cards, tarpaulin, janitorial supplies,and the like which were not GAD related.

Entries were not taken up by the Accounting Office. A separate registry for the fund was still not maintained. Goods for distribution were still recorded as expenses rather than inventories. Also, no lists of recipients were attached to the said disbursements.




The MOOE balance at year end amounted to ₱2,654,682.97. However, this amount was not yet transferred to the Trust Fund books.

observations.managementAction:
Management appointed a new ICO - Municipal Treasurer on January 4, 2016.  He installed stricter internal controls over the municipality’s cash management system.

Seven job order personnel were applied for fidelity bond.

The windows at the Treasurer’s Office are strictly accessed by clients for collection activities only. Vendors were restricted to sell their products inside the municipal building.  

Partition was already constructed between the Treasurer’s Office and Accounting Office.

The RIS and RAAF were regularly prepared by the collectors.  




The OIC- Municipal Treasurer and collectors religiously remitted and deposited collections daily including the verification of used and unused accountable forms.

There was no appointed/ designated Assistant Municipal Treasurer.  The OIC- Municipal Treasurer performed the duties and functions of Liquidating Officer such as monitoring and checking of collections and accountable forms.
observations.reason: It is the OIC- Municipal Treasurer who acts as custodian of accountable form at the same time.
